request.question: What actor has appeared in the films "Hamlet", "The Woodlanders" and "The Holiday"?

title: Peter Ellis (actor)
Christopher Fairbank
The Holiday
Cesco Baseggio
Rufus Sewell
John Barrymore
Franz Matter
Skylar Astin
Gregory Peck
Alan Cumming
sentences: Peter Ellis (born 30 May 1936) is an English actor.
Peter has worked extensively in theatre and television both as an actor and director.He spent four seasons with the RSC.
Three years with the Old Vic Co including playing Benvolio in Zeffirelli's "Romeo and Juliet".
Hotspur in "Henry IV" and later Rosencrantz in a world tour of "Hamlet" starring Derek Jacobi.
He also played Guildenstern in "Hamlet" on the site of the new Globe Theatre.
Five years at the Crucible Sheffield.
In the West End he appeared in "The Tulip Tree" and in Ray Cooney's "Funny Money".
He is perhaps best known for playing the role of Chief Superintendent Charles Brownlow in the long running ITV drama "The Bill".
Has also appeared as a semi-regular in "Emmerdale Farm" and "Coronation Street".
Other series include "Sam" "Lyttons Diary", "Edward and Mrs Simpson", "The XYY Man", "Nolan", "The Les Dawson Show", Victoria Wood's play "Talent" for Granada and "Acorn Antiques" for BBC TV.
He appeared in the David Mercer television play "In Two Minds" (1967), a work which was directed by Ken Loach.
Christopher "Chris" Fairbank (born 4 October 1953) is an English film, stage and television actor, perhaps best known for his role as Moxey in the hit comedy-drama series "Auf Wiedersehen, Pet".
Born in Hertfordshire, he first developed an interest in acting while appearing in a play at his primary school in Clavering, Essex.
He has numerous television credits to his name, including "Sapphire and Steel", "The Professionals", and "The Scarlet Pimpernel".
He provided voice talent for both of the Wallace and Gromit feature-length films, "Curse of the Were-Rabbit" and "Flushed Away".
Fairbank also appeared as one of the pair of muggers who rob an out-of-town family, heralding the first appearance of the Batman in Tim Burton's 1989 film.

Fairbank also had roles as Mactilburgh the scientist in the film "The Fifth Element," the prisoner Murphy in "Alien 3", and the Player Queen in the Franco Zeffirelli version of "Hamlet", opposite Mel Gibson.
He appeared in the "Goal!
" trilogy as a Newcastle United fan.
The Holiday is a 2006 American romantic comedy film written, produced and directed by Nancy Meyers.

Co-produced by Bruce A. Block, it was filmed in both California and England, and stars Cameron Diaz and Kate Winslet as Amanda and Iris, two lovelorn women from opposite sides of the Atlantic Ocean, who temporarily exchange homes to escape heartbreak during the holiday season.
Jude Law and Jack Black were cast as the film's leading men Graham and Miles, with Eli Wallach, Shannyn Sossamon, Edward Burns and Rufus Sewell playing key supporting roles.
Francesco "Cesco" Baseggio (1897–1971) was an Italian stage, film and television actor.
He was born in Venice, and was identified with Venetian roles during his film career.
He appeared in a mixture of serious, dramatic films, as well as comedies such as "The Brambilla Family Go on Holiday" (1941).
On the stage he frequently appeared in plays by Carlo Goldoni.
Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.

In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".

On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).
In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".
In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".
He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.
On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.
John Barrymore (born John Sidney Blyth; February 14 or 15, 1882 – May 29, 1942) was an American actor on stage, screen and radio.

A member of the Drew and Barrymore theatrical families, he initially tried to avoid the stage, and briefly attempted a career as an artist, but appeared on stage together with his father Maurice in 1900, and then his sister Ethel the following year.

He began his career in 1903 and first gained attention as a stage actor in light comedy, then high drama, culminating in productions of "Justice" (1916), "Richard III" (1920) and "Hamlet" (1922); his portrayal of Hamlet led to him being called the "greatest living American tragedian".
Franz Matter (1931–1999) was a Swiss actor and film director.
A noted stage actor, Matter also appeared in a number of films including the 1958 literary adaptation "The Cheese Factory in the Hamlet".
He also worked as an assistant director, and directed the 1961 film "Rosen auf Pump".
Skylar Astin Lipstein (born September 23, 1987), known professionally as Skylar Astin, is an American actor and singer.
He became known for portraying Jesse Swanson in the musical films "Pitch Perfect" (2012) and "Pitch Perfect 2" (2015).

He was also in the original cast of the Broadway musical "Spring Awakening", and has since appeared in films such as "Hamlet 2" (2008), "Taking Woodstock" (2009), "Cavemen" (2013), and "21 & Over" (2013).
Eldred Gregory Peck (April 5, 1916 – June 12, 2003) was an American actor who was one of the most popular film stars from the 1940s to the 1960s.
Peck continued to play major film roles until the late 1980s.
His performance as Atticus Finch in the 1962 film "To Kill a Mockingbird" earned him the Academy Award for Best Actor.
He had also been nominated for an Oscar for the same category for "The Keys of the Kingdom" (1944), "The Yearling" (1946), "Gentleman's Agreement" (1947) and "Twelve O'Clock High" (1949).

Other notable films he appeared in include "Spellbound" (1945), "Roman Holiday" (1953), "Moby Dick" (1956, and its 1998 miniseries), "Pork Chop Hill" (1959), "The Guns of Navarone" (1961), "Cape Fear" (1962, and its 1991 remake), "How the West Was Won" (1962), "The Omen" (1976) and "The Boys from Brazil" (1978).
Alan Cumming, OBE (born 27 January 1965), is a Scottish-American actor, singer/performer, author, and activist who has appeared in numerous films, television shows, and plays.

His London stage appearances include "Hamlet", the Maniac in "Accidental Death of an Anarchist" (for which he received an Olivier Award), the lead in "Bent", and the National Theatre of Scotland's "The Bacchae".
On Broadway, he has appeared in "The Threepenny Opera", as the master of ceremonies in "Cabaret" (for which he won a Tony Award), "Design for Living" and a one-man adaptation of "Macbeth".
His best-known film roles include his performances in "Emma", "GoldenEye", the "Spy Kids" trilogy, "Son of the Mask," and "X2".
Cumming also introduces "Masterpiece Mystery!
" for PBS and appeared on "The Good Wife", for which he has been nominated for three Primetime Emmy Awards, two Screen Actors Guild Awards, two Golden Globe Awards and a Satellite Award.
A filming of his Las Vegas cabaret show, "Alan Cumming Sings Sappy Songs", aired on PBS stations in November 2016.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What actor has appeared in the films "Hamlet", "The Woodlanders" and "The Holiday"?
hop1_docs: Borthwood | Borthwood is a hamlet on the Isle of Wight, adjacent to Borthwood Copse, the National Trust woodland.  Borthwood includes some holiday cottages and a pet kennel.  At the 2011 Census the Post Office specified that the population of the hamlet was included in the civil parish of Newchurch, Isle of Wight.

Andrzej Fedorowicz (actor) | Andrzej Fedorowicz (born 21 January 1942) is a Polish actor.  He has made over 10 appearances in film.  He starred in the 1978 comedy film "What Will You Do When You Catch Me? ".

Creagen Dow | Creagen Dow (born May 1, 1991) is an American/Canadian actor, writer, producer who had a recurring role as Jeremiah Trottman on the Nickelodeon series "Zoey 101".  He has also guest starred on "The Big Bang Theory", "Rizzoli & Isles", "Hannah Montana", "Castle", "Entourage", "Hot in Cleveland" and "", to name a few.  Creagen received a Best Supporting Actor award for his role in the feature film "Hamlet's Ghost" and also appeared alongside Reese Witherspoon and Vince Vaughn in "Four Christmases", with Sean Astin in "Amazing Love" and provided the voice of "Mullet Boy" in the film "The Ant Bully".  Creagen can also be seen in various television commercials including the GEICO Horror Movie - It's What You Do campaign.

Rufus Sewell | Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.  In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".  On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).  In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".  In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".  He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.  On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.

Linda Regan | Linda Regan (born 1949) is a British author and actress, who has appeared in television and film.  She is best known for her role as Yellowcoat April in the British holiday camp sitcom "Hi-de-Hi! ".  She is married to the actor Brian Murphy.

Mike Estime | Mike Estime is an American actor and comedian.  He has made appearances in the film "Last Holiday" (2006) and the sitcom "Talk to Me" (2000) as well as the hit television series "Everybody Hates Chris" as Risky, a street entrepreneur, and on the BET comedy show "Comic View".

Frank Campeau | Frank Campeau (December 14, 1864 – November 5, 1943) was an American actor.  He appeared in 93 films between 1911 and 1940 and made many appearances in films starring Douglas Fairbanks.  He was born in Detroit, Michigan and died in Woodland Hills, Los Angeles.
response.summary_1:
Actor candidate: Rufus Sewell

- Supported facts from passages:
  - Rufus Frederik Sewell: "is an English actor." (passage [4])
  - He "appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of 'Hamlet' (1996) playing Fortinbras." (passage [4])
  - He "has appeared in ... 'The Woodlanders'." (passage [4])

- Missing / ambiguous facts blocking the answer:
  - Missing fact: no passage confirms who appeared in "'The Holiday'." Bridge entity to check: Rufus Sewell (candidate who matches the first two films).
  - Possible ambiguity: multiple films titled "Hamlet" exist; passage explicitly cites the 1996 Kenneth Branagh version.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. "Rufus Sewell" — check lead/infobox and full filmography section for a listing of "The Holiday".
  2. "The Holiday (2006 film)" — open Cast/Production/Credits sections to see full cast list.
  3. "The Woodlanders (film) cast" — verify Rufus Sewell's role there (if needed).
  4. Web/search query: "Rufus Sewell The Holiday cast" or "Rufus Sewell filmography The Holiday" for corroboration.
response.hop2_query: Rufus Sewell The Holiday cast appearance
hop2_docs: The Holiday | The Holiday is a 2006 American romantic comedy film written, produced and directed by Nancy Meyers.  Co-produced by Bruce A. Block, it was filmed in both California and England, and stars Cameron Diaz and Kate Winslet as Amanda and Iris, two lovelorn women from opposite sides of the Atlantic Ocean, who temporarily exchange homes to escape heartbreak during the holiday season.  Jude Law and Jack Black were cast as the film's leading men Graham and Miles, with Eli Wallach, Shannyn Sossamon, Edward Burns and Rufus Sewell playing key supporting roles.

Rufus Sewell | Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.  In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".  On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).  In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".  In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".  He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.  On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.

Holidays Rule | Holidays Rule is a collection of holiday music featuring various artists that range from indie rock to jazz and pop, including Paul McCartney, Rufus Wainwright, The Shins.  The songs on the compilation are both traditional and modern.  The album was released by Hear Music/Concord Music Group on October 30, 2012 in North America.  Chris Funk of The Decemberists served as the album’s producer, and he also appears on the album with Black Prairie.

Tristan & Isolde (film) | Tristan & Isolde is a 2006 epic romantic drama film directed by Kevin Reynolds and written by Dean Georgaris based on the medieval romantic legend of Tristan and Isolde.  Produced by Ridley Scott (who had been working on an adaptation since the mid-1970s) and Tony Scott, the film stars James Franco and Sophia Myles, alongside a supporting cast featuring Rufus Sewell, Mark Strong, and Henry Cavill.  This was Franchise Pictures' last film before bankruptcy.

All Things to All Men (film) | All Things to All Men is a British film written and directed by George Isaac.  It stars Gabriel Byrne, Rufus Sewell, Toby Stephens, and Julian Sands.  Sewell plays a dirty cop who manipulates both the underworld and police in order to entrap a thief.

Dangerous Beauty | Dangerous Beauty is a 1998 American biographical drama film directed by Marshall Herskovitz and starring Catherine McCormack, Rufus Sewell, and Oliver Platt.  Based on the non-fiction book "The Honest Courtesan" by Margaret Rosenthal, the film is about Veronica Franco, a courtesan in sixteenth-century Venice who becomes a hero to her city, but later becomes the target of an inquisition by the Church for witchcraft.  The film features a supporting cast that includes Fred Ward, Naomi Watts, Moira Kelly and Jacqueline Bisset.  The film was released as A Destiny of Her Own in some regions, and was retitled The Honest Courtesan for its UK video release.

William C.B. Sewell House | William C. B. Sewell House, also known as the Sewell-Freese House, is a historic home located at Covington, Fountain County, Indiana.  It was built in 1867, and is a 2 1/2-story, three bay, Italianate style brick dwelling.  It has two wings and a separate summer kitchen.  The front facade features a full-width, one-story decorative front porch and a pair of cast-iron lions.
response.summary_2: {"summary":"The actor is Rufus Sewell. Rufus Sewell is an English actor who played Fortinbras in Kenneth Branagh’s Hamlet (1996), appeared in The Woodlanders, and is listed among the supporting cast of Nancy Meyers’s The Holiday (2006)."}
